facts.text: The NES helped revive the video game industry in North America after the 1983 crash.
The NES was originally released in Japan as the Famicom in 1983.
The NES introduced the D-pad controller design that became an industry standard.
The NES used cartridges instead of floppy disks or CDs.
Nintendo implemented strict quality control with the 'Seal of Quality' on NES games.
The Atari 2600 popularized home console gaming in the late 1970s.
The Atari 2600 used switch-based hardware controls on the console itself.
The Atari 2600 was one of the first consoles to use interchangeable cartridges.
The Atari 2600 helped define the concept of third-party game development.
The Atari 2600 hardware had only 128 bytes of RAM.
The SNES introduced Mode 7 graphics for rotation and scaling effects.
The SNES used a 16-bit CPU compared to the NES's 8-bit processor.
The SNES was released as the Super Famicom in Japan in 1990.
The SNES controller introduced shoulder buttons (L and R).
The SNES had advanced audio capabilities via a dedicated sound chip.
The Sega Genesis was marketed with the slogan 'Genesis does what Nintendon't'.
The Sega Genesis is a 16-bit console released in 1988 in Japan.
The Genesis had a faster CPU than the SNES but weaker graphics capabilities.
The Genesis helped popularize aggressive marketing in the console wars.
The Sega CD was an add-on that expanded Genesis capabilities using CD-ROMs.
The PlayStation 1 popularized CD-based gaming over cartridges.
The PS1 was originally developed as a Nintendo CD add-on before becoming standalone.
The PS1 used 3D polygon graphics at a time when most consoles were 2D.
The PlayStation brand became one of the most successful console lines in history.
The PS1 memory card allowed portable game saves for the first time for many players.
The Nintendo 64 introduced analog stick control for 3D movement.
The N64 used cartridges instead of CDs, which limited storage but improved load times.
The N64 controller had an unusual three-prong design.
The Nintendo 64 was released in 1996 in North America.
The N64 was one of the first consoles to support four controller ports built-in.
The Dreamcast was the first console with built-in online multiplayer capabilities.
The Dreamcast used proprietary GD-ROM discs instead of standard CDs.
The Dreamcast VMU functioned as both a memory card and a mini-screen device.
The Dreamcast was Sega’s final home console.
The Dreamcast had strong arcade-style hardware influences.
The PlayStation 2 is the best-selling console of all time.
The PS2 could play DVDs, making it a popular entertainment device.
The PS2 had a custom Emotion Engine CPU.
The PS2 had backward compatibility with most PS1 games.
The PS2 helped standardize DVD playback in living rooms.
The Xbox introduced Microsoft into the console market in 2001.
The original Xbox included a built-in hard drive.
Xbox Live helped establish modern console online services.
The Xbox controller was famously large and nicknamed 'The Duke'.
Halo: Combat Evolved was the Xbox’s breakout launch title.
The Nintendo Wii introduced motion-controlled gaming to a mainstream audience.
The Wii Remote used accelerometers to detect movement.
Wii Sports became one of the most popular bundled games in history.
The Wii was part of Nintendo’s strategy to expand beyond traditional gamers.
The Wii introduced a simplified, family-friendly gaming interface.
The PlayStation 2 is the best-selling console of all time with over 155 million units sold worldwide.
The PS2 doubled as a DVD player, which helped drive its massive adoption in living rooms.
The PS2 supported backward compatibility with most original PlayStation games.
The PlayStation 2 used the Emotion Engine CPU designed for advanced 3D graphics processing.
The PS2 helped standardize DVD media as a core part of console entertainment.
The original PlayStation sold over 102 million units worldwide, making it a breakthrough success for Sony.
The PlayStation popularized CD-ROM gaming and reduced reliance on cartridges.
The PS1 helped establish Sony as a dominant force in the gaming industry.
The PlayStation introduced memory cards as a standard method for saving game progress.
The PS1 originated from a failed collaboration project between Sony and Nintendo.
The Nintendo DS became the best-selling handheld system of all time with over 154 million units sold.
The Nintendo DS introduced dual screens with a resistive touchscreen on the bottom display.
The DS helped popularize touch-based gaming long before smartphones dominated the market.
The DS supported local wireless multiplayer without requiring internet access.
The Nintendo DS was backward compatible with Game Boy Advance cartridges.
The Nintendo Switch has sold over 140 million units worldwide.
The Switch is a hybrid console that functions as both a handheld and home console.
The Joy-Con controllers support motion controls and HD rumble feedback.
The Switch replaced the commercially unsuccessful Wii U in Nintendo’s lineup.
The Nintendo Switch became one of the fastest-selling consoles in Nintendo’s history.
The Xbox 360 sold over 84 million units worldwide and defined Microsoft’s early console success.
Xbox Live on the Xbox 360 helped establish modern online console ecosystems.
The Xbox 360 introduced achievements as a cross-game progression system.
The Xbox 360 became infamous for hardware failures known as the Red Ring of Death.
The Xbox 360 helped establish Microsoft as a major competitor in gaming.
The Nintendo Wii sold over 101 million units worldwide.
The Wii introduced motion-based gameplay to mainstream audiences.
Wii Sports became one of the most influential bundled games ever released.
The Wii targeted casual and non-traditional gamers with simplified controls.
The Wii Remote used accelerometers to detect motion and gestures.
The Nintendo 3DS sold over 75 million units worldwide.
The Nintendo 3DS featured glasses-free stereoscopic 3D technology.
The 3DS included an analog Circle Pad for improved 3D gameplay control.
The 3DS was backward compatible with Nintendo DS games.
The Nintendo 3DS eShop enabled digital game downloads and indie titles.
The Sega Genesis sold over 30 million units worldwide and defined the 16-bit era.
The Genesis competed directly with the Super Nintendo during the console wars.
Sonic the Hedgehog was created as Sega’s answer to Mario.
The Sega Genesis used a Motorola 68000 CPU.
The Genesis became known for its aggressive marketing campaigns in the 1990s.
The Atari 2600 sold over 30 million units worldwide and popularized home gaming.
The Atari 2600 helped establish the cartridge-based home console model.
The Atari 2600 used a simple joystick with a single action button.
The Atari 2600 contained only 128 bytes of RAM.
The Atari 2600 played a major role in the early video game industry boom.
The Sega Dreamcast sold around 9 million units worldwide before being discontinued.
The Dreamcast was Sega’s final home console.
The Dreamcast included built-in online multiplayer support at launch.
The Dreamcast VMU acted as both a memory card and a mini LCD device.
The Dreamcast used GD-ROM discs to increase storage capacity over CDs.
The PlayStation 3 sold over 87 million units worldwide.
The PS3 introduced Blu-ray as a standard gaming media format.
The PS3 launched with a high-end Cell processor architecture designed for parallel processing.
The PlayStation Network enabled digital downloads and online multiplayer on PS3.
The PS3 initially launched at a premium price point of $599 in the US.
The Xbox One sold over 58 million units worldwide.
The Xbox One initially focused heavily on multimedia and TV integration features.
The Xbox One originally required an always-online connection before policy changes were reversed.
The Xbox One launched with Kinect integration as a core feature.
Xbox Live helped establish Microsoft’s long-term online gaming ecosystem.
The Nintendo Switch has sold over 140 million units worldwide.
The Switch is a hybrid console that works as both a handheld and home system.
The Joy-Con controllers support motion controls and HD rumble feedback.
The Switch replaced the commercially unsuccessful Wii U.
The Nintendo Switch became one of Nintendo’s fastest-selling consoles globally.
The Wii sold over 101 million units worldwide.
The Wii introduced motion-controlled gaming to a mainstream audience.
Wii Sports became one of the most influential bundled games in history.
The Wii was designed to appeal to non-traditional gamers and families.
The Wii Remote used accelerometer technology to detect motion.
The Nintendo 3DS sold over 75 million units worldwide.
The Nintendo 3DS featured glasses-free stereoscopic 3D visuals.
The 3DS included a Circle Pad for analog control input.
The 3DS was backward compatible with Nintendo DS game cartridges.
The Nintendo 3DS eShop allowed digital game purchases and indie titles.
The Sega Genesis sold over 30 million units worldwide during the 16-bit era.
The Genesis competed directly with the Super Nintendo Entertainment System.
Sonic the Hedgehog was created as Sega’s mascot to rival Mario.
The Genesis used a Motorola 68000 CPU architecture.
The Genesis is remembered for aggressive marketing during the console wars.
The Atari 2600 sold over 30 million units worldwide and defined early home gaming.
The Atari 2600 helped establish interchangeable cartridge gaming.
The Atari 2600 used a joystick with a single action button.
The Atari 2600 had only 128 bytes of RAM.
The Atari 2600 played a major role in the early growth of the video game industry.
The Sega Dreamcast sold around 9 million units worldwide before being discontinued.
The Dreamcast was Sega’s final home console system.
The Dreamcast supported online multiplayer gaming at launch.
The Dreamcast VMU acted as both a memory card and a mini display device.
The Dreamcast used GD-ROM discs for higher storage capacity than CDs.
The PlayStation 4 sold over 117 million units worldwide.
The PS4 emphasized social sharing, streaming, and online integration.
The DualShock 4 introduced a built-in touchpad.
The PS4 helped Sony regain dominance in the console market.
The PS4 became a major platform for indie game development.
The PlayStation 5 has sold over 60 million units worldwide.
The PS5 features an ultra-fast SSD for near-instant load times.
The DualSense controller introduced adaptive triggers and advanced haptics.
The PS5 supports ray tracing for advanced lighting effects.
The PlayStation 5 continues Sony’s dominance in modern console gaming.
The Xbox Series X sold over 25 million units worldwide in its early lifecycle.
The Xbox Series X was designed for high-performance 4K gaming.
The Xbox Series X features a custom SSD for fast load times.
The Xbox Series X supports backward compatibility with multiple generations of Xbox games.
Xbox Game Pass became a defining feature of the Xbox ecosystem.
The Nintendo GameCube sold over 21 million units worldwide.
The GameCube used miniDVD discs instead of standard DVDs.
The GameCube controller design is still praised for its ergonomic feel.
The GameCube introduced a handle on the console for portability.
The GameCube was Nintendo’s first console to use optical discs exclusively.
The Nintendo Entertainment System (NES) sold over 61 million units worldwide.
The NES revived the home video game industry after the 1983 crash.
The NES introduced iconic franchises like Super Mario Bros. and The Legend of Zelda.
The NES used a lockout chip to enforce game licensing control.
The NES controller design influenced decades of future gamepads.
The Super Nintendo Entertainment System sold over 49 million units worldwide.
The SNES introduced Mode 7 graphics for rotation and scaling effects.
The SNES featured a 16-bit CPU and advanced audio hardware for its time.
The SNES controller introduced shoulder buttons that became standard.
The SNES is considered one of the greatest consoles of the 16-bit era.
The Sega Saturn sold around 9 million units worldwide.
The Saturn struggled against the PlayStation due to complex architecture.
The Sega Saturn featured strong 2D capabilities for arcade-style games.
The Saturn had dual CPU architecture that made development difficult.
The Saturn was popular in Japan but struggled in Western markets.
The Neo Geo AES was known as a luxury console with arcade-perfect ports.
The Neo Geo AES cartridges were extremely expensive compared to other systems.
The Neo Geo hardware was based on arcade machine architecture.
The Neo Geo is famous for fighting games like King of Fighters and Samurai Shodown.
The Neo Geo AES is now a highly collectible retro console.
The Atari Jaguar marketed itself as the first 64-bit console.
The Atari Jaguar struggled commercially despite technical ambitions.
The Jaguar controller featured a large number keypad.
The Atari Jaguar sold under 2 million units worldwide.
The Jaguar CD add-on extended the console’s capabilities but had limited success.
The Sega Master System sold over 13 million units worldwide.
The Master System was more successful in Europe than in North America.
The Master System had better hardware than the NES in some areas.
The Master System used both cartridges and Sega Cards for games.
The Master System helped establish Sega as a major console competitor.
The PlayStation 5 has sold over 60 million units worldwide.
The PS5 features an ultra-fast SSD that dramatically reduces load times.
The DualSense controller introduced adaptive triggers and advanced haptics.
The PS5 supports ray tracing for advanced lighting and reflections.
The PS5 continues Sony’s leadership in modern console gaming.
The Magnavox Odyssey was the first commercial home video game console.
The Magnavox Odyssey released in 1972 and had no sound capabilities.
The Odyssey used physical overlays on television screens for gameplay visuals.
The Magnavox Odyssey helped create the entire home console industry.
The Odyssey is considered the origin point of modern video game consoles.
